title: Reaction enthalpy and Hess's law
definition: The heat released or absorbed by a reaction at constant pressure, per mole of reaction: $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$. A **negative** value is exothermic (heat flows out); a **positive** value is endothermic. Because enthalpy is a state function, $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}$ depends only on the substances, not the path — **Hess's law** — so it can be assembled from tabulated **standard enthalpies of formation** $\Delta H_f^\circ$: $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = \sum \nu\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu\,\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$, where an element in its standard state has $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ by definition. The tabulated value is **per mole of reaction**; the heat an actual run delivers is $q = \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}\cdot\xi$, scaled by the extent $\xi$ the limiting reagent allows — the energy is the extent ledger's other column.
